text: Combine the 1D kinematic equations you already know to predict where a launched projectile goes in two dimensions.

pretrieval.displayText: Before the lesson, make a hunch: when a projectile is in the air, which part of its motion does gravity directly change?
pretrieval.explanation: The lesson will separate horizontal and vertical motion so you can use the right equation for each direction.
options: Gravity directly changes only the horizontal velocity.
Gravity directly changes only the vertical velocity while horizontal velocity stays constant.
Gravity changes horizontal and vertical velocity by the same amount.
Gravity has no effect after launch because the projectile already has speed.

steps.displayText: In this lesson you'll learn how to do 2D kinematics using the 1D kinematic equations you already know. We'll fire cannons, decompose velocities into horizontal and vertical pieces, and predict flight time, height, and landing distance.
Fire all three cannons. Each launches at the same speed but a different angle (30, 45, and 60 degrees). Watch which one travels the farthest before deciding why.

You already have two 1D equations. Vertically, height follows y = vt - gt^2 as gravity pulls the ball back down. Horizontally, distance follows x = vt at constant speed. Combining them gives a full 2D model of a launched object.

A cannon fires at angle theta = 45 degrees with speed v = 30 m/s. Using information about right triangles (hint: it involves sin and cosine), enter the horizontal and vertical velocity components. Round each to the nearest tenth (m/s).
steps.explanation: Decompose the velocity along the triangle: vx = v*cos(theta) = 30*cos(45 degrees) = 21.2 m/s and vy = v*sin(theta) = 30*sin(45 degrees) = 21.2 m/s. At 45 degrees the components are equal.
steps.hint: Split the velocity with the right triangle: vx = v*cos(theta) and vy = v*sin(theta).

steps.displayText: A cannonball is fired at theta = 30 degrees with speed v = 20 m/s. How long (in seconds) until it returns to its starting elevation? Hint: you only need to know it's vertical trajectory. Round to the nearest tenth (s).
steps.explanation: Set the vertical displacement to zero: 0 = vy*t - (1/2)g*t^2, so t = 2*vy/g. With vy = 20*sin(30 degrees) = 10 m/s and g = 9.8, t = 2*10/9.8 = 2.0 s.
steps.hint: Only vertical motion sets the time. Find vy = v*sin(theta), then t = 2*vy/g for a same-height landing.

steps.displayText: A cannonball is fired at theta = 60 degrees with speed v = 20 m/s. Drag the bar to predict the maximum height it reaches, then press Fire cannonball. Round to the nearest meter.
steps.explanation: At the top, vertical velocity is zero. Using vy^2 = 2*g*H, H = vy^2/(2g). With vy = 20*sin(60 degrees) = 17.3 m/s, H = 17.3^2/(2*9.8) = 15 m.
steps.hint: At the top, vertical velocity is 0. Find vy = v*sin(theta), then H = vy^2/(2*g).

steps.displayText: A cannon on a 25 m cliff fires at theta = 45 degrees with speed v = 20 m/s. Predict how far from the base of the cliff the ball lands. If you're stuck, consider that only the vertical trajectory impacts time in air, while horizontal distance is only dependent on time. Round to the nearest meter.
steps.explanation: First find time to land: 0 = 25 + vy*t - (1/2)g*t^2 with vy = 20*sin(45 degrees) = 14.1 m/s gives t = 4.12 s. Then x = vx*t = 14.1*4.12 = 58 m.
steps.hint: Vertical motion sets the time, horizontal sets the distance. Solve 0 = h0 + vy*t - (1/2)g*t^2 for t, then x = vx*t.

retrieval.displayText: Retrieval practice: a cannon fires at theta = 30 degrees with speed v = 40 m/s. Enter the horizontal and vertical velocity components, rounded to the nearest tenth (m/s).
retrieval.explanation: Use vx = v*cos(theta) = 40*cos(30 degrees) = 34.6 m/s and vy = v*sin(theta) = 40*sin(30 degrees) = 20.0 m/s.
retrieval.hint: Use vx = v*cos(theta) and vy = v*sin(theta).

retrieval.displayText: Retrieval practice: a cannonball is fired at theta = 30 degrees with speed v = 30 m/s. How long until it returns to its starting elevation? Round to the nearest tenth (s).
retrieval.explanation: The vertical speed is vy = 30*sin(30 degrees) = 15 m/s. For a same-height landing, t = 2*vy/g = 30/9.8 = 3.1 s.

retrieval.displayText: Retrieval practice: a cannonball is fired at theta = 30 degrees with speed v = 24 m/s. Predict its maximum height, rounded to the nearest meter.
retrieval.explanation: The vertical speed is vy = 24*sin(30 degrees) = 12 m/s. At the top, H = vy^2/(2g) = 144/19.6 = 7.3 m, so about 7 m.
retrieval.hint: Find vy = v*sin(theta), then H = vy^2/(2*g).

retrieval.displayText: Retrieval practice: a cannon on a 20 m cliff fires at theta = 37 degrees with speed v = 25 m/s. Predict how far from the cliff base it lands, rounded to the nearest meter.
retrieval.explanation: The vertical equation gives a flight time of about 4.1 s. The horizontal speed is vx = 25*cos(37 degrees) = 20.0 m/s, so x = vx*t = 20.0*4.1 = 81 m.
retrieval.hint: Solve the vertical equation for the flight time, then x = vx*t.

retrieval.displayText: Retrieval practice: a cannonball is fired at theta = 60 degrees with speed v = 18 m/s. How long until it returns to its starting elevation? Round to the nearest tenth (s).
retrieval.explanation: The vertical speed is vy = 18*sin(60 degrees) = 15.6 m/s. For a same-height landing, t = 2*vy/g = 31.2/9.8 = 3.2 s.
